landing.title: Are you a good person?
landing.label: Test
landing.subtitle: God's Law. Eight questions. One verdict.

resumeDialog.title: Pick up where you left off?

test.counterLabel: died since you opened this page

verdict.deathLineTemplate: people died while you answered. You could be next.
verdict.confessionAdmitted: You are {list} — by your own admission.
verdict.confessionBoth: You are {admitted} — by your own admission. And {denied} — by your evasions.

questions.text: Have you ever told a lie — even a small one?
questions.commandment: You shall not bear false witness
questions.followUp: What about a 'white lie' to spare someone's feelings?
questions.honestFollowUp: Even one lie. A 'white lie,' an exaggeration, a half-truth — by God's standard, that makes you a liar.

questions.text: Have you ever taken something that wasn't yours — even something small?
questions.followUp: Have you ever kept extra change, or downloaded something you didn't pay for?
questions.honestFollowUp: Regardless of value — a pen, someone's time, credit for work that wasn't yours. The value doesn't matter. The act does.

questions.text: Have you ever looked at someone with lust?
questions.followUp: Jesus said, 'Whoever looks at a woman to lust for her has already committed adultery with her in his heart.' — Matthew 5:28 (NKJV)
questions.honestFollowUp: Jesus said that looking with lust is adultery of the heart. Not by culture's standard — by God's.

questions.text: Have you ever been so angry you wished someone harm?
questions.followUp: Even a flash of hatred — Jesus said, 'Whoever is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment.' — Matthew 5:22 (NKJV)
questions.honestFollowUp: Jesus equated unjust anger with murder. The hatred in your heart is enough.

questions.text: Have you ever used God's name as a curse word?
questions.commandment: You shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain
questions.followUp: Even 'OMG' as an exclamation — using the Creator's name carelessly?
questions.honestFollowUp: You used the name of the God who gave you life as a throwaway word. That is blasphemy.

questions.text: Have you ever wanted something that belonged to someone else?
questions.followUp: A friend's lifestyle, someone's relationship, a coworker's promotion?
questions.honestFollowUp: Coveting reveals a heart that says 'what God has given me is not enough.' It's ingratitude made personal.

questions.text: Have you ever put something — money, career, relationships — above God?
questions.commandment: You shall have no other gods before Me
questions.followUp: If you've spent more time on your phone than thinking about your Creator...
questions.honestFollowUp: Anything you love, trust, or serve more than God is an idol. It doesn't have to be a statue.

questions.text: Have you ever failed to honor your parents?
questions.commandment: Honor your father and your mother
questions.followUp: Even in your heart — resentment, dismissiveness, ingratitude?
questions.honestFollowUp: Not just outward obedience — God sees the resentment, the eye-rolls, the dismissiveness in your heart.

verdict.title: Guilty.
verdict.subtitle: By God's perfect standard, none of us are good enough.

grace.scripture: For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life.
grace.label: Grace

beats.headline: You're guilty. The fine is eternal.
beats.subtitle: You've broken God's Law — not once, but repeatedly. And the penalty for sin is not a fine you can pay. It's death.

beats.headline: Imagine standing before a just Judge. You can't pay. Justice demands death.
beats.subtitle: A good judge can't let a guilty criminal walk free. And God is the ultimate good Judge. Your guilt must be answered for.

beats.headline: But someone steps into the courtroom and pays your fine in full.
beats.subtitle: Every penny. The judge can now legally dismiss your case — not because he's overlooking justice, but because justice has been satisfied.

beats.headline: Jesus Christ — God in flesh — lived the life you couldn't, died the death you deserved, and rose.
beats.subtitle: Two thousand years ago, the Creator entered His creation. He lived perfectly, took your punishment on the cross, and three days later conquered death itself.

beats.headline: Repent — turn from your sin. Trust in Christ alone. God will forgive you and grant you eternal life.
beats.subtitle: This isn't about a prayer or being religious. It's about turning from sin and placing your trust in the One who paid it all.

invitation.heading: What will you do with this?
invitation.thinkingEncouragement: Think it through honestly — that's fair. Just don't let 'later' quietly become 'never'. No one is promised tomorrow.
invitation.dismissedEncouragement: The door stays open — but no one is promised tomorrow to walk through it.

responses.committed: I will repent and trust in Christ
responses.thinking: I want to think about it

share.whatsappMessage: Are you a good person? Take this test and find out:
share.telegramMessage: Are you a good person? Take this test and find out:

nextSteps.dismissedReturn: Changed your mind? The reading plan is waiting — start today.

trackA.welcomeReturn: The decision is made. This is how it takes root.
trackA.whatHappened:
You acknowledged that you've broken God's Law. And you've made the decision to repent — to turn from your sin — and to place your trust in Jesus Christ alone.

If you've genuinely repented and trusted in the Saviour, God has forgiven every sin you've ever committed and granted you eternal life. Not because you earned it — but because of what Christ did on the cross.

"Therefore, if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; old things have passed away; behold, all things have become new." — 2 Corinthians 5:17 (NKJV)
trackA.readBody: Start with the Gospel of John — it's the best place to meet Jesus for the first time. Or follow our 7-day reading plan.
trackA.prayBody: Talk to God like a Father. He's listening.
trackA.prayPrompt: God, thank You for forgiving me. Help me to understand what You've done for me. Guide me as I read Your Word. Give me the courage to turn from sin and follow You. In Jesus' name, Amen.
trackA.communityBody: You weren't meant to do this alone. Find a Bible-believing church where you can grow.
trackA.learnBody: Explore the foundations of the faith — who Jesus is, what grace means, why the cross matters.
trackA.shareMessage: I made a decision that changes everything:

trackB.acknowledgment: That's honest. Here are some things worth thinking about.
trackB.acknowledgmentReturn: Still thinking it through — good. These are worth weighing.
reflections: If what you heard is true, what would that change for you?
Have you ever considered that the discomfort you feel might be your conscience agreeing with God's standard?
What would it take for you to investigate this further?
trackB.readingBody: Read John chapter 3 — today, not someday. It's a conversation between Jesus and a man who had questions just like yours.
trackB.learnBody: Short, honest explanations of the core questions — who Jesus claimed to be, why it matters, what grace is.
trackB.comeBack: Come back to this — but don't shelve it. It's never too late while you're breathing. It's never safe to count on another chance.

readingPlan.heading: 7-Day Reading Plan
readingPlan.subtitle: A week in the Gospel of John — meeting Jesus for yourself.
readingPlan.allCompleteBody: You've finished the 7-day plan. But this is just the beginning — keep reading, keep praying, keep growing. The Gospel of John has 21 chapters. Why not keep going?
readingPlan.deeperLink: Explore the foundations of what you've been reading.
readingPlan.nextStepsLabel: Walking with Christ is more than reading.
readingPlan.resetConfirmBody: This will clear all 7 days of progress. You can start again from Day 1 — nothing else is affected.

days.title: The Beginning
days.keyVerse: And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.
days.reflection: Before Jesus was born in Bethlehem, He existed eternally as God. He wasn't created — He was the Creator who stepped into His creation. The same God whose Law convicted you is the God who became a man to save you.
days.prayer: God, help me to understand who Jesus really is — not just a good teacher, but the Word made flesh. Open my eyes as I read. Amen.

days.title: Born Again
days.keyVerse: Jesus answered and said to him, 'Most assuredly, I say to you, unless one is born again, he cannot see the kingdom of God.'
days.reflection: Nicodemus was a religious leader — a 'good person' by anyone's standard. But Jesus told him that being good wasn't enough. You need a completely new start. That's what happened when you trusted Christ — you were born again.
days.prayer: Lord, thank You for giving me a new beginning. Help me to live as someone who has been born again — not trying to earn Your love, but resting in it. Amen.

days.title: Living Water
days.keyVerse: Jesus answered and said to her, 'Whoever drinks of this water will thirst again, but whoever drinks of the water that I shall give him will never thirst.'
days.reflection: Jesus met this woman at her lowest — ashamed, alone, avoiding others. He didn't condemn her. He offered her something that would satisfy forever. He meets you the same way — wherever you are, however you feel.
days.prayer: Jesus, I've tried to fill my life with things that don't last. Be my living water. Satisfy the thirst that nothing else can. Amen.

days.title: The Good Shepherd
days.keyVerse: I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd gives His life for the sheep.
days.reflection: A hired hand runs when danger comes. But Jesus — the Good Shepherd — willingly laid down His life. Nobody took it from Him. He chose the cross because He chose you.
days.prayer: Good Shepherd, thank You for not running when it cost You everything. Help me to hear Your voice and follow where You lead. Amen.

days.title: The Way
days.keyVerse: Jesus said to him, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me.'
days.reflection: This is the most exclusive and the most inclusive claim ever made. Exclusive — there's only one way. Inclusive — anyone can take it. Jesus didn't say 'I'll show you the way.' He said 'I AM the way.'
days.prayer: Lord, in a world of many voices and many paths, give me confidence that You are the way. Help me to trust You even when I don't understand. Amen.

days.title: The Vine
days.keyVerse: I am the vine, you are the branches. He who abides in Me, and I in him, bears much fruit; for without Me you can do nothing.
days.reflection: The Christian life isn't about trying harder. It's about staying connected. A branch doesn't strain to produce fruit — it just stays attached to the vine. Your job is to abide. Stay close. Read. Pray. Trust.
days.prayer: Jesus, teach me what it means to abide in You — not to perform for You, but to remain in Your love and let You work through me. Amen.

days.title: Risen
days.keyVerse: Jesus said to him, 'Thomas, because you have seen Me, you have believed. Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'
days.reflection: Thomas doubted. Jesus didn't reject him — He showed up. If you have doubts, bring them to God. He's not afraid of your questions. The resurrection is the foundation of everything — if Jesus rose, then everything He said is true.
days.prayer: Risen Lord, strengthen my faith. When doubts come, remind me that You are alive and that Your promises are sure. Help me to live boldly for You. Amen.

learn.label: Learn
learn.hubSubtitle: Explore the foundations of the gospel — one topic at a time.
learn.resetConfirmBody: This will clear all topic completions and quiz answers. You can start exploring again from the beginning.

topics.title: Am I a Good Person?
topics.subtitle: The question everyone answers too quickly.
topics.metaDescription: Am I a good person? Most people say yes — here's how to actually find out.

sections.heading: Everyone thinks they're good
sections.body:
Ask almost anyone, 'Are you a good person?' and you'll hear the same answer: 'I think so. I mean, I'm not perfect, but...' And there it is — the comparison. Not perfect compared to what?

Most of us measure ourselves against other people. We pick someone worse — a criminal on the news, a neighbour who cheats, a dictator from a history book — and next to them we look decent. It's grading on a curve, and on that curve nearly everyone passes.

But goodness isn't a curve. When a rich young ruler called Jesus 'Good Teacher,' Jesus stopped him cold: 'Why do you call Me good? No one is good but One, that is, God.' The standard isn't other people. It never was.
sections.scripture: So Jesus said to him, 'Why do you call Me good? No one is good but One, that is, God.'

quiz.question: What standard do most people measure their goodness by?
options: Other people
God's Law
Their conscience alone
quiz.reveal: We instinctively compare ourselves with people worse than us — and pass. But God doesn't grade on a curve. His standard is His own perfect character.

sections.heading: The mirror of God's Law
sections.body:
So how do you measure yourself against God's standard? He wrote it down. The Ten Commandments are not a ladder you climb to reach heaven — they're a mirror. You look into them and see yourself as you actually are.

A mirror does one thing well: it shows you the dirt on your face. But you would never try to wash your face with the mirror. It can reveal the problem; it cannot fix it.

That is exactly what the Bible says the Law is for: 'by the law is the knowledge of sin.' Its job is diagnosis, not cure. And an honest diagnosis is the one thing most of us have never sat still for.
sections.scripture: Therefore by the deeds of the law no flesh will be justified in His sight, for by the law is the knowledge of sin.

quiz.question: According to Romans 3:20, what is the Law's purpose?
options: To make us good
To show us our sin
To earn our way to heaven
quiz.reveal: The Law is a mirror, not a ladder. 'By the law is the knowledge of sin.' It reveals the problem — it was never designed to fix it.

sections.heading: Take the test honestly
sections.body:
So look in the mirror. Have you ever told a lie? Even one, even a small one — what does that make you? Have you ever taken something that didn't belong to you, whatever its value? Have you ever hated someone? The Bible says 'whoever hates his brother is a murderer.'

If you're honest, you've just admitted — like the rest of us — to being a lying thief with murder in the heart. And that's only three of the Ten Commandments.

Maybe you're thinking, 'But I've kept most of the others.' James closes that door. God's Law is like a pane of glass: crack it in one corner and the whole pane is broken. Stumble in one point, and you are guilty of all.
sections.scripture: For whoever shall keep the whole law, and yet stumble in one point, he is guilty of all.

quiz.question: You've broken one commandment but kept the other nine. What does James 2:10 say about you?
options: You're ninety percent good
You're guilty of breaking the whole Law
It depends how serious the one was
quiz.reveal: Stumble in one point and you are 'guilty of all.' God's Law isn't a score out of ten — it's a pane of glass, and one crack breaks the whole thing.

sections.heading: Good by whose standard?
sections.body:
Here is where many people reach for one last hope: 'But God is good. Surely a good God will overlook my failures.' Think that through in a courtroom.

Imagine a judge with a guilty murderer standing before him. The evidence is overwhelming. And the judge says, 'I'm a good man, so I'm letting you go.' Is he good? He's corrupt. A good judge cannot wave crime through — precisely because he is good, he must see justice done.

God's goodness is not your escape route. It is the very reason your case must come to court. The Bible says that acquitting the guilty is an abomination to Him. If God is good — and He is — then He must judge lying, theft, and hatred. Including yours and mine.
sections.scripture: He who justifies the wicked, and he who condemns the just, both of them alike are an abomination to the LORD.

quiz.question: Does the fact that God is good help the guilty on judgment day?
options: Yes — a good God will overlook sin
No — a good judge must judge
It depends on your good deeds
quiz.reveal: A judge who lets the guilty walk free isn't good — he's corrupt. God's goodness is the very reason sin cannot be waved through. That is what makes the cross necessary.

sections.heading: Find out where you stand
sections.body:
So — are you a good person? By man's standard, maybe. By God's standard, you already know the answer. So do the rest of us, because we're standing in the same dock.

The Bible doesn't tell you to assume you're fine. It says, 'Examine yourselves.' Not your neighbour. Not the person the news calls a monster. Yourself, against the standard that actually counts.

This site has a short test that walks you through God's Law one commandment at a time, the way Jesus used it. It won't save you — no test can. But it will tell you the truth about where you stand. And no one looks for a cure before facing the diagnosis.
sections.scripture: Examine yourselves as to whether you are in the faith. Test yourselves. Do you not know yourselves, that Jesus Christ is in you?—unless indeed you are disqualified.

faq.question: How good do you have to be to go to heaven?
faq.answer: God's standard is moral perfection — Jesus said, 'Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect.' Measured against the Ten Commandments, no one clears that bar: one lie makes a liar, one theft a thief. That is why the gospel is not advice to try harder but the news that Christ kept the standard and paid for those who broke it.

faq.question: Does being sincere or religious make someone good enough?
faq.answer: Sincerity cannot cancel guilt — a sincere debtor still owes the debt. Scripture says all our righteous acts are like filthy rags before a holy God. Religion polishes the outside; the courtroom problem remains. What God requires is repentance and trust in the One who paid the fine, not a fuller record of religious effort.

faq.question: If nobody is good, what hope is there?
faq.answer: The verdict is meant to drive you to the rescue, not to despair. God demonstrated His love in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us. Those who repent and put their trust in Him pass from death to life — not because they became good, but because His goodness is counted as theirs.

topics.title: Who is Jesus?
topics.subtitle: More than a good teacher.
topics.metaDescription: Who was Jesus of Nazareth? Explore the evidence for His deity, humanity, and what it means for you.

sections.heading: More than a good teacher
sections.body:
Most people are happy to call Jesus a good teacher. But Jesus didn't leave that option open. He claimed to be God — not a god, not godlike, but God Himself. 'I and My Father are one' (John 10:30).

C.S. Lewis put it simply: a man who said the sort of things Jesus said would not be a great moral teacher. He would either be a lunatic, a liar, or He would be exactly who He claimed to be — the Lord.

The people who heard Him understood exactly what He was claiming. They picked up stones to kill Him for blasphemy. You don't stone someone for saying 'be kind to your neighbour.'
sections.scripture: I and My Father are one.

quiz.question: If Jesus claimed to be God, what are the only logical options?
options: He was a great moral teacher
He was a lunatic, a liar, or He is Lord
He was speaking metaphorically
quiz.reveal: A mere teacher doesn't claim to be God. Either He was delusional, deliberately deceiving, or telling the truth. C.S. Lewis called this the 'Lord, Liar, or Lunatic' trilemma — there is no fourth option.

sections.heading: The Word made flesh
sections.body:
The Gospel of John opens with a staggering claim: 'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.' And then: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.'

Before Bethlehem, before the manger, before Mary — Jesus existed eternally as God. He wasn't created. He was the Creator who stepped into His own creation. The hands that shaped the universe let themselves be nailed to a cross.
sections.scripture: And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we beheld His glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father, full of grace and truth.

sections.heading: What He did
sections.body:
Jesus lived the only sinless life in human history. He healed the sick, gave sight to the blind, and raised the dead. He had authority over nature, disease, demons, and death itself.

But He didn't come primarily to heal or teach. He came to die. He told His disciples repeatedly that He would be handed over, crucified, and rise on the third day. His death was not an accident or a tragedy — it was the plan from the beginning.
sections.scripture: Who, being in the form of God, did not consider it robbery to be equal with God, but made Himself of no reputation, taking the form of a bondservant, and coming in the likeness of men.

sections.heading: Why it matters to you
sections.body:
If Jesus is who He said He is — God in the flesh — then everything He said carries the weight of divine authority. When He said 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me,' He wasn't offering one option among many. He was stating a fact.

If He rose from the dead (and the evidence is compelling), then His claims are validated. Death could not hold Him. And if death could not hold Him, then His promise of eternal life to all who trust in Him is not wishful thinking — it's the most reliable promise ever made.
sections.scripture: Jesus said to him, 'I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me.'

quiz.question: If Jesus truly rose from the dead, what does that mean about His claims?
options: They're inspiring but unverifiable
They are validated — He is who He said He is
It doesn't change anything
quiz.reveal: The resurrection is the ultimate proof. If death could not hold Him, then His promise of eternal life is the most reliable promise ever made.

faq.question: Did Jesus really claim to be God?
faq.answer: Yes — plainly. He said, 'I and My Father are one,' and the people who heard Him understood exactly what He meant: they picked up stones to kill Him for blasphemy. No one stones a man for saying 'be kind to your neighbour.' C. S. Lewis argued the claim leaves only three options: Jesus was a lunatic, a liar, or exactly who He said He was — Lord.

faq.question: Was Jesus just a good moral teacher?
faq.answer: Not by His own words. A merely good teacher does not claim eternal existence or divine authority. John's Gospel opens by calling Jesus the Word who 'was with God, and was God' before becoming flesh among us. He lived a sinless life, healed the sick, and raised the dead — evidence He was not offering advice but revealing who He is.

faq.question: Why does it matter whether Jesus rose from the dead?
faq.answer: If Jesus rose, His claims are validated — death could not hold Him, and He told His disciples in advance that He would be crucified and rise on the third day. That means His words carry divine authority, including His claim to be the only way to the Father. His promise of eternal life to those who trust Him rests on a resurrection, not wishful thinking.

topics.title: What is Sin?
topics.subtitle: Why God's standard matters — and why none of us meet it.
topics.metaDescription: What does the Bible mean by 'sin'? Understand God's moral standard and why every person falls short.

sections.heading: God's standard
sections.body:
The Ten Commandments are not suggestions. They are God's moral law — a perfect reflection of His character. They tell us what is right and what is wrong, not based on culture or opinion, but based on the nature of God Himself.

You shall not lie. You shall not steal. You shall not commit adultery. You shall not covet. You shall have no other gods before Me. These aren't arbitrary rules — they reveal what a holy God requires of the beings He created in His image.
sections.scripture: For whoever shall keep the whole law, and yet stumble in one point, he is guilty of all.

quiz.question: According to James 2:10, how many commandments do you need to break to be guilty?
options: All ten
A majority of them
Just one
quiz.reveal: Just one. 'Whoever shall keep the whole law, and yet stumble in one point, he is guilty of all.' God's standard is perfection, not improvement.

sections.heading: It's not just murder and theft
sections.body:
Jesus raised the bar impossibly high. He said that anger without cause is the same as murder in God's eyes. That looking at someone with lust is the same as adultery. That hating your brother makes you a murderer at heart.

Sin is not just what you do — it's what you think, what you desire, what you harbour in secret. God doesn't just see your actions. He sees your heart. And by that standard, every single one of us is guilty.
sections.scripture: But I say to you that whoever looks at a woman to lust for her has already committed adultery with her in his heart.

sections.heading: Everyone has sinned
sections.body:
Romans 3:23 does not say 'some have sinned' or 'really bad people have sinned.' It says all. Every human being who has ever lived — except Jesus — has broken God's Law.

This is not about being 'good enough.' It's about being perfect — because that is God's standard. And none of us are. Not the religious leader, not the moral philosopher, not the person who volunteers every weekend. All have sinned.
sections.scripture: For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.

quiz.question: According to God's standard, how good do you need to be to enter heaven?
options: Better than most people
Good enough to outweigh your bad
Perfect
quiz.reveal: Perfect. God's standard isn't 'good enough' — it's moral perfection. And 'all have sinned and fall short.' That's why we need a Saviour.

sections.heading: The consequence
sections.body:
Sin is not a minor infraction. It is cosmic treason against the Creator of the universe. And the penalty is not a slap on the wrist — it is death. Not just physical death, but eternal separation from God.

This is the reality that makes the gospel urgent. If sin had no consequence, there would be no need for a Saviour. But sin does have a consequence. And it is permanent. That is why what comes next — what God did about it — is the best news you will ever hear.
sections.scripture: For the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

faq.question: What does the Bible actually mean by sin?
faq.answer: Sin is falling short of God's moral law — not a matter of culture or opinion, but of His unchanging character. It is not only what you do; Jesus said that anger without cause is equivalent to murder and lust is equivalent to adultery in God's eyes. Sin is what you think and desire in secret as much as what you visibly do, because God weighs the heart, not just the record.

faq.question: Is everyone really a sinner, even good people?
faq.answer: Romans 3:23 does not say 'some' or 'the really bad ones' — it says all have sinned. Every person who has ever lived, except Jesus, has broken God's Law at some point. The bar is not 'good enough,' since God's standard is perfection. That includes the religious leader, the moral philosopher, and the person who volunteers every weekend — nobody clears it on their own.

faq.question: What actually happens because of sin?
faq.answer: Sin is not a minor infraction to shrug off — the Bible calls it cosmic treason against the Creator, and its wages are death, not just physical but eternal separation from God. That consequence is what makes the gospel urgent: if sin carried no penalty, there would be nothing to be saved from. What God did about that penalty is the reason it counts as good news.

topics.title: Why the Cross?
topics.subtitle: How justice and mercy meet at Calvary.
topics.metaDescription: Why did Jesus have to die on the cross? Understand how God's justice and love come together in the crucifixion.

sections.heading: The problem
sections.body:
God faces what seems like an impossible dilemma. He is perfectly just — He cannot ignore sin, sweep it under the rug, or pretend it didn't happen. A good judge doesn't let guilty criminals walk free.

But God is also perfectly loving. He does not want anyone to perish. He created you, knows you, and loves you. So how can a just God forgive guilty sinners without compromising His justice?
sections.scripture: He is the Rock, His work is perfect; for all His ways are justice, a God of truth and without injustice; righteous and upright is He.

sections.heading: The courtroom
sections.body:
Imagine you're standing before a judge, guilty of a serious crime. The fine is enormous — far more than you could ever pay. Justice demands payment. You're about to be sentenced.

But then someone walks into the courtroom, opens their wallet, and pays your fine in full. Every penny. The judge can now legally dismiss your case — not because he's overlooking justice, but because justice has been satisfied. The fine has been paid.

That is what happened on the cross.
sections.scripture: But He was wounded for our transgressions, He was bruised for our iniquities; the chastisement for our peace was upon Him, and by His stripes we are healed.

quiz.question: In the courtroom analogy, why can the judge legally let you go?
options: Because He decided to overlook your crimes
Because someone paid your fine in full
Because you promised to be better
quiz.reveal: Justice was satisfied — not ignored. Someone paid the penalty you owed. That's substitutionary atonement: Christ took your punishment so God could legally forgive you.

sections.heading: What happened on the cross
sections.body:
On the cross, Jesus took upon Himself the punishment that every human being deserves. He absorbed the full wrath of God against sin — not His own sin (He had none), but ours.

God 'made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us.' The sinless One became the sin-bearer. He experienced the full weight of divine judgment so that we wouldn't have to. This is not a metaphor. This is substitution — He died in your place.
sections.scripture: For He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him.

sections.heading: It is finished
sections.body:
Jesus' last words on the cross were not 'I am defeated.' They were 'It is finished.' In Greek, the word is tetelestai — a legal and commercial term meaning 'paid in full.'

The debt is settled. There is nothing you can add to what Christ has done. You cannot earn salvation by being good, going to church, or doing charitable works. Those are good things — but they are not payment. The payment has already been made. All that remains is for you to receive it.
sections.scripture: So when Jesus had received the sour wine, He said, 'It is finished!' And bowing His head, He gave up His spirit.

quiz.question: What does 'It is finished' (tetelestai) mean?
options: 'I give up'
'Paid in full'
'The beginning'
quiz.reveal: Tetelestai was a legal and commercial term meaning 'paid in full.' The debt of sin is settled. There is nothing you can add to what Christ has done.

faq.question: Why did Jesus have to die instead of God just forgiving sin?
faq.answer: Because forgiveness that ignores justice isn't mercy, it's corruption — a good judge cannot wave crime through, however loving he is. God is both perfectly just and perfectly loving, so the debt sin incurs had to be paid, not pretended away. On the cross, Christ paid that fine in full, so God could dismiss the case legally, not by looking the other way.

faq.question: What actually happened to Jesus on the cross?
faq.answer: Jesus took on Himself the punishment every sinner deserves, absorbing the full weight of God's judgment against sin — not His own, since He had none, but ours. Scripture says God 'made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us.' This is substitution, not metaphor: the sinless One stood in the place of the guilty, so guilty people could go free.

faq.question: Does 'it is finished' mean there's nothing left for me to do?
faq.answer: It means the payment is complete — tetelestai was a commercial term for 'paid in full,' and no amount of good works, church attendance, or charity can add to a debt that is already settled. What remains isn't paying more; it's receiving what was paid. Those who repent and trust in what Christ finished are the ones the payment actually covers.

topics.title: How Can My Sins Be Forgiven?
topics.subtitle: Why 'I'll do better' isn't enough — and what is.
topics.metaDescription: How can my sins be forgiven? Not by good deeds, rituals, or promises to do better — see what the Bible says about how forgiveness really works.

sections.heading: Why being good now can't fix it
sections.body:
When the weight of your sins finally lands, the first plan almost everyone reaches for is the same: 'From now on, I'll do better.' It sounds noble. It changes nothing about the past.

Imagine a man caught stealing who tells the judge, 'I haven't stolen anything for six months.' The judge would answer, 'Good — that's what the law always required of you. Now what about everything you stole before?' Living right from today onward earns no credit against yesterday's debt, because obedience was never extra. It was owed all along.

And there's a deeper problem still. The Bible says that even our righteous deeds — our best days, not our worst — are like filthy rags before a holy God, because they come from hearts already stained. Good works cannot clean a guilty record. If your sins are ever going to be forgiven, the answer has to come from outside you.
sections.scripture: But we are all like an unclean thing, and all our righteousnesses are like filthy rags; we all fade as a leaf, and our iniquities, like the wind, have taken us away.

quiz.question: A thief promises the judge he'll never steal again. What does that do about what he already stole?
options: Nothing — the debt for the past still stands
It clears his record if he keeps his word
It counts as partial payment
quiz.reveal: Obeying from today onward was always owed — it earns no credit against yesterday. Future goodness can't pay a past debt. That's why 'I'll do better' is not forgiveness.

sections.heading: The fine must be paid
sections.body:
Then why doesn't God simply let it go? Because forgiveness that ignores justice isn't goodness — it's corruption. If a judge dismissed proven crimes with a shrug, no one would praise his mercy. Sin carries a real penalty, and the Bible names it without flinching: the wages of sin is death.

Now picture the courtroom. You're guilty. The fine is astronomical — more than you could earn in ten lifetimes. You have nothing. And then someone steps forward and says, 'I'll pay it. All of it.' Once the fine is paid, the judge can let you go — legally, justly, with the law fully satisfied.

That is the only shape forgiveness can take for you. Your fine doesn't shrink with time, tears, or good behaviour. Either you pay it — and the payment is death — or someone pays it in your place.
sections.scripture: For the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

quiz.question: Why can't God simply overlook your sins?
options: Because He's waiting to see if you improve
Because a good judge must see justice done
Because your sins are too great to forgive
quiz.reveal: A judge who waves proven crimes through isn't merciful — he's corrupt. God's justice demands that the fine be paid. Forgiveness had to find a way to pay it, not ignore it.

sections.heading: What happened at the cross
sections.body:
Someone did step forward. On a cross outside Jerusalem, Jesus Christ suffered once for sins — 'the just for the unjust.' He wasn't paying His own fine; He had never sinned. He was paying yours.

If you've read about why the cross had to happen, you know this was no tragedy that spun out of control. It was a payment, planned and priced. God's justice demanded death for sin, and the Son of God stood in your place in the dock and took the sentence Himself — so that He might bring you to God.

Which means the payment for your sins is not hypothetical. It exists, it is complete, and nothing can be added to it. The question left standing is not 'How could I ever pay?' — you never could. It is 'How do I receive what He paid?'
sections.scripture: For Christ also suffered once for sins, the just for the unjust, that He might bring us to God, being put to death in the flesh but made alive by the Spirit.

quiz.question: In what sense did Jesus die 'the just for the unjust'?
options: As a moral example for us to admire
As a substitute, paying the penalty in our place
To show that suffering is unavoidable
quiz.reveal: Substitution is the heart of the cross: the sinless One took the sentence of the guilty. Your fine was paid in full by someone else — and that is the only reason forgiveness is possible.

sections.heading: Repentance and faith
sections.body:
So how do you receive it? Notice first what the Bible does not say. It doesn't say 'perform this ritual.' It doesn't say 'repeat these exact words.' It doesn't say 'attend church and hope it adds up.' No ceremony, formula, or membership washes away a single sin — if it did, forgiveness would be a machine you operate, and God would owe you the result. He owes you nothing. Forgiveness is a gift, and gifts are received, never triggered.

What God commands is repentance and faith — two sides of one turning. Repentance: you stop excusing your sin, agree with God that it is wrong, and turn from it. Faith: you trust the person of Jesus Christ — not ideas about Him — the way a man jumping from a plane trusts a parachute. It's not enough to believe it works; you have to put it on.

Neither of these earns anything. A drowning man doesn't earn his rescue by grabbing the lifeguard's hand — he simply stops trusting his own swimming. Repent and trust the Saviour, 'that your sins may be blotted out.'
sections.scripture: Repent therefore and be converted, that your sins may be blotted out, so that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord.

quiz.question: Which of these can wash away your sins?
options: Baptism or a word-perfect prayer
Regular church attendance and good works
None of these — forgiveness is received through repentance and faith in Christ
quiz.reveal: No ritual or formula obliges God. Forgiveness was purchased at the cross, and it is received — not earned, not triggered — by turning from sin and trusting the Saviour.

sections.heading: What now
sections.body:
Start by being honest with God — today, in your own words. Confession is not a performance and needs no polished language. It means agreeing with God about your sin: naming it plainly, without excuses, the way you'd level with a doctor you actually wanted to cure you.

Notice how the promise is worded: 'If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us.' If. The promise is real, and it is conditional — it belongs to those who confess and turn and trust, not to those who merely read about it. Reading about forgiveness no more forgives you than reading about bread feeds you.

If you're not sure whether any of this really applies to you, don't guess. Take the test on this site and let God's Law show you where you stand. Then pick up a Bible — the Gospel of John is a good place to start — and go to God as you are. No one who has ever come to Him like that has been turned away.
sections.scripture: If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.

faq.question: Can I just try to be a better person to make up for my past?
faq.answer: No — living rightly from today only meets what was always required; it adds no credit against yesterday's debt, since obedience was never optional extra credit. Even our best deeds are described in Scripture as filthy rags before a holy God, because they come from a heart already stained. If your sins are ever forgiven, the answer has to come from outside your own effort.

faq.question: Is there a ritual or prayer that guarantees forgiveness?
faq.answer: No formula, ceremony, or set of words washes away sin — if it did, forgiveness would be a machine you operate rather than a gift you receive. What God commands is repentance and faith: turning from sin and trusting the person of Jesus Christ, the way someone jumping from a plane trusts the parachute, not just believes it works. Neither of those earns anything; both simply receive what Christ already paid.

faq.question: How do I know if I'm actually forgiven?
faq.answer: Scripture states the promise with a condition: 'If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us.' It belongs to those who confess, turn from sin, and trust Christ — not to those who merely read about it. If you're unsure this describes you, be honest with God today in your own words rather than guessing, and let that honesty be the starting point.

topics.title: What is Repentance?
topics.subtitle: More than feeling sorry — a complete change of direction.
topics.metaDescription: What does it really mean to repent? Learn the biblical definition of repentance and what it looks like in practice.

sections.heading: Not just feeling sorry
sections.body:
Judas felt sorry for betraying Jesus. He was so filled with regret that he returned the thirty pieces of silver and hanged himself. But the Bible never calls that repentance. It calls it remorse.

Remorse is feeling bad about the consequences of sin. Repentance is feeling grieved about the sin itself — because it offends a holy God. Remorse says 'I wish I hadn't been caught.' Repentance says 'I was wrong, and I want to change.'
sections.scripture: For godly sorrow produces repentance leading to salvation, not to be regretted; but the sorrow of the world produces death.

quiz.question: What is the difference between remorse and repentance?
options: They are the same thing
Remorse regrets consequences; repentance grieves the sin itself
Repentance is stronger regret
quiz.reveal: Judas had remorse — he regretted what happened. But repentance is godly sorrow that leads to change. 'I was wrong, and I want to turn from it.'

sections.heading: Turn and trust
sections.body:
Repentance has two parts. First, you turn FROM sin — not just the sins you're comfortable admitting, but the whole orientation of your life away from God. Second, you turn TO God — placing your trust in Jesus Christ and His finished work on the cross.

It's not about cleaning yourself up before coming to God. You come as you are — broken, guilty, empty-handed. And He receives you. Repentance is not a prerequisite for grace. It is the response to grace.
sections.scripture: Repent therefore and be converted, that your sins may be blotted out, so that times of refreshing may come from the presence of the Lord.

quiz.question: Do you need to clean yourself up before coming to God?
options: Yes — you need to be ready first
No — you come as you are
Only for serious sins
quiz.reveal: You come as you are — broken, guilty, empty-handed. Repentance is not a prerequisite for grace. It is the response to grace.

sections.heading: What it looks like
sections.body:
Repentance is not perfection. It is a new direction. A repentant person still stumbles, still fails, still sins. But they fall forward. They get back up. They hate what they used to love and love what they used to ignore.

Think of the prodigal son. He didn't clean himself up before going home. He came covered in pig filth, reeking of failure. And his father ran to him, embraced him, and restored him. That is the picture of repentance — coming home, just as you are.
sections.scripture: And he arose and came to his father. But when he was still a great way off, his father saw him and had compassion, and ran and fell on his neck and kissed him.

sections.heading: It's a gift
sections.body:
Here is the most surprising thing about repentance: even the ability to repent is a gift from God. You cannot manufacture it. You cannot work it up through willpower or religious effort.

If you feel a pull toward God right now — a desire to turn, to change, to come home — that is not coming from you. That is God drawing you to Himself. Don't resist it. Ask Him for the gift of repentance, and He will give it.
sections.scripture: When they heard these things they became silent; and they glorified God, saying, 'Then God has also granted to the Gentiles repentance to life.'

faq.question: What's the difference between being sorry and actually repenting?
faq.answer: Judas felt sorry for betraying Jesus — filled with regret, he returned the silver and hanged himself, yet the Bible never calls that repentance. It calls it remorse: feeling bad about consequences. Repentance is grief over the sin itself, because it offends a holy God, and it leads somewhere remorse doesn't — toward turning, not just toward regret. Remorse says 'I wish I hadn't been caught'; repentance says 'I was wrong, and I want to change.'

faq.question: Do I need to clean up my life before I can come to God?
faq.answer: No — repentance is not a prerequisite you complete before grace; it's the response grace produces. Like the prodigal son, who came home covered in pig filth rather than cleaning himself up first, you come to God as you are: broken, guilty, empty-handed. He receives you there. Repentance has two sides — turning from sin and turning toward trusting Christ — and both happen after you come, not before.

faq.question: Can I make myself repent through willpower?
faq.answer: No — even the ability to repent is described in Scripture as a gift from God, not something manufactured by effort or religious discipline. If you feel a pull toward God right now, a desire to turn and come home, that pull isn't coming from you; it's God drawing you. The response is not to resist it or try harder, but to ask Him for the gift and receive it.

topics.title: What Happens When You Die?
topics.subtitle: The question everyone asks — and the Bible's answer.
topics.metaDescription: What happens when you die? See what the Bible says about death, the judgment that follows, heaven and hell — and what death cannot touch for those in Christ.

sections.heading: The appointment
sections.body:
The Bible is blunt about death. It is not a mystery to be avoided or a topic to dance around. 'It is appointed for men to die once, but after this the judgment.' Not if — when. Not maybe — certainly.

Every culture, every religion, every philosophy has tried to answer the question of what comes after death. Reincarnation, annihilation, wishful vagueness. But the Bible gives a clear, unambiguous answer: you die once, and then you face God.
sections.scripture: And as it is appointed for men to die once, but after this the judgment.

sections.heading: Two destinations
sections.body:
Jesus spoke more about hell than anyone else in the Bible. He described it as a place of outer darkness, weeping, and gnashing of teeth — eternal separation from God and everything good.

He also spoke about heaven — the presence of God, where there is no more death, no more pain, no more tears. A place prepared for those who belong to Him.

There is no middle ground. No purgatory, no second chance after death, no cosmic do-over. Two roads, two destinations. And the road you're on is determined by what you do with Jesus.
sections.scripture: And these will go away into everlasting punishment, but the righteous into eternal life.

quiz.question: According to the Bible, is there a middle ground between heaven and hell?
options: Yes — purgatory or a second chance
No — two destinations, no middle ground
It depends on how you lived
quiz.reveal: No middle ground. No purgatory, no second chance after death. Two roads, two destinations — and the road is determined by what you do with Jesus.

sections.heading: The death counter is real
sections.body:
1.8 people die every second. That's not a statistic designed to frighten you — it's a reality designed to wake you up. The death counter on this site isn't a gimmick. It's a mirror.

Every one of those numbers was a person — with a name, a family, hopes, fears. And every one of them has now stepped into eternity. One of those numbers, someday, will be yours. The only question is: are you ready?
sections.scripture: So teach us to number our days, that we may gain a heart of wisdom.

sections.heading: But there is hope
sections.body:
God does not want anyone to perish. He is 'not willing that any should perish but that all should come to repentance.' He made a way — the cross. Jesus bridged the gap between a holy God and sinful humanity.

The question is not whether God has provided a way. He has. The question is whether you will take it. Trust in Jesus Christ. Turn from sin. Receive the gift of eternal life. The counter is still ticking — but it doesn't have to be a countdown to judgment. It can be a countdown to coming home.
sections.scripture: For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life.

quiz.question: Has God provided a way to be saved from judgment?
options: No — we must save ourselves
Yes — through Jesus Christ and the cross
Only for certain people
quiz.reveal: God made a way. The question is not whether He provided it — He has. The question is whether you will take it. Trust in Christ. Turn from sin. Receive eternal life.

sections.heading: The courtroom after
sections.body:
What follows death is not a vague reckoning or a lingering sense of consequence. It is an appearance. 'We must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ' — all of us, no exemptions, no absentees. The Bible describes it the way you would describe a court date: a place, a Judge, a record.

And consider what you can bring into that courtroom: nothing. No lawyer to spin the facts, no crowd to compare yourself against, no chance to edit the record. Everything 'done in the body' is already in evidence — the deeds everyone saw and the ones no one did.

This is why this site keeps talking about a verdict. Not to be dramatic — because the Bible is. There is a real courtroom after death, and you will stand in it. And the Judge will be Jesus Christ Himself, which means the One appointed to judge you is the same One who came to save you.
sections.scripture: For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, that each one may receive the things done in the body, according to what he has done, whether good or bad.

quiz.question: What does 2 Corinthians 5:10 say about the judgment seat of Christ?
options: Only the worst offenders will appear before it
We must all appear before it
It is a metaphor for our conscience
quiz.reveal: All of us. No exemptions, no absentees, nothing left off the record. And the Judge is the same Jesus who came to save — which is either the worst news you have ever heard, or the best.

sections.heading: What death cannot touch
sections.body:
Now hear what Jesus says about the person who has heard His word, turned from sin, and trusted the Father who sent Him: that person 'shall not come into judgment, but has passed from death into life.' Not will pass — has passed. For that person, the courtroom holds no terror, because the case was settled at the cross before they ever arrived.

This is what death cannot touch. It can stop a heart; it cannot undo a pardon. For the one who has repented and trusted in Christ, dying is no longer a step into the unknown — it is a step into the presence of the One who already knows them.

Notice what this promise is not. It is not for everyone automatically, and it does not become yours by reading it on a page. It belongs to the one who hears and believes — really believes, with the kind of trust that turns a whole life around. The promise is solid. The only question is whether it describes you.
sections.scripture: Most assuredly, I say to you, he who hears My word and believes in Him who sent Me has everlasting life, and shall not come into judgment, but has passed from death into life.

quiz.question: In John 5:24, who has 'passed from death into life'?
options: Everyone, no matter how they respond to Jesus
The one who hears Jesus' word and believes the Father who sent Him
No one can know until the judgment
quiz.reveal: The promise is conditional — it belongs to the one who hears and believes. For that person, judgment is already settled and death has lost its grip. It is solid; the question is whether it describes you.

sections.heading: Today is the day
sections.body:
Here is a plain fact, stated without any tricks: you do not know the date of your own death. Almost no one does. The people who died in the last hour woke up expecting an ordinary day.

That is not a scare tactic — it is simply why 'later' is a dangerous answer to the most important question you will ever face. Later is not a time you can live in. Every decision you have ever made, you made today. This one is no different.

God's timing is more generous than fear suggests and more urgent than comfort would like: 'now is the accepted time; behold, now is the day of salvation.' Not after you have cleaned yourself up. Not once you are old. Now — while your heart is still beating and the door is still open. If God is speaking to you through what you have read, don't file it away for a better moment. There isn't one.
sections.scripture: For He says: 'In an acceptable time I have heard you, And in the day of salvation I have helped you.' Behold, now is the accepted time; behold, now is the day of salvation.

faq.question: What does the Bible say happens right after you die?
faq.answer: Hebrews 9:27 is blunt: 'it is appointed for men to die once, but after this the judgment.' Not maybe — certainly. The Bible rules out reincarnation, annihilation, and vague second chances, describing instead an appearance before Christ's judgment seat, where everything done in the body is already in evidence. There is no middle ground and no do-over after that point.

faq.question: Is there a second chance after death, like purgatory?
faq.answer: No — Scripture describes two destinations only, heaven and hell, with no purgatory and no cosmic do-over. Jesus spoke of hell as everlasting separation from God and of heaven as His presence, where there is no more death or pain. The road you're on is determined by what you do with Jesus before death, not by anything offered afterward.

faq.question: How can I not fear dying?
faq.answer: Jesus said that the one who hears His word and believes, really believes, in the Father who sent Him 'has passed from death into life' and will not come into judgment. Death can stop a heart, but it cannot undo that pardon — for the one who has repented and trusted Christ, dying becomes a step into His presence, not into the unknown. The promise is real, but it belongs to those it actually describes.

topics.title: Does God Exist?
topics.subtitle: You have more evidence than you think.
topics.metaDescription: Does God exist? Weigh three lines of evidence — creation, conscience, and Christ — and face the real reason the question stays unsettled.

sections.heading: The building has a builder
sections.body:
How do you know a building had a builder? You've never seen him. You don't know his name. There's no photo of him in the lobby. Yet you're certain he existed, because the building is standing there. Buildings don't assemble themselves.

A painting is proof of a painter. A car is proof of a maker. And creation — from the sun that warms your face to the DNA coiled inside every one of your cells — is evidence of a Creator. That's not a leap in the dark. It's the same everyday reasoning you trust a hundred times a day.

This is the Bible's starting point. It never argues for God in the abstract; it points at what He made. His invisible attributes 'are clearly seen' through the things He created — so clearly, Paul says, that no one will stand before God with the excuse 'I didn't know.'
sections.scripture: For since the creation of the world His invisible attributes are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even His eternal power and Godhead, so that they are without excuse.

quiz.question: How do you know a building had a builder?
options: You take it on blind faith
The building itself is the evidence
You can't know without meeting him
quiz.reveal: You've never seen the builder, but the building is all the evidence you need. Creation works the same way — made things point to a Maker.

sections.heading: Your conscience is evidence
sections.body:
There's a second witness, and it's closer than the stars. It's inside you. The word 'conscience' means 'with knowledge' — when you do wrong, you do it with knowledge that it's wrong.

Every culture in history has known that murder is evil, that betrayal is shameful, that courage and honesty are good. We argue about the edges, but no one seriously claims cruelty to a child is fine for those who happen to enjoy it. Some things aren't opinions. They're known.

But a moral law that stands above every culture needs a source above every culture. If right and wrong were human inventions, we could vote them out of existence. We can't — and deep down you know it. The Bible tells you why: the work of God's Law is written on your heart.
sections.scripture: Who show the work of the law written in their hearts, their conscience also bearing witness, and between themselves their thoughts accusing or else excusing them.

quiz.question: What does Romans 2:15 say is written on every human heart?
options: Nothing — we're born blank
The work of God's Law
Whatever our culture teaches us
quiz.reveal: Your conscience isn't a social invention. It's the work of God's Law written on your heart, bearing witness every time you do wrong 'with knowledge.'

sections.heading: God stepped into history
sections.body:
If God had left only fingerprints — stars and consciences — you might still wonder what He's like. Kind? Distant? Angry? Fingerprints tell you someone was there; they don't show you a face.

So He showed us His face. 'No one has seen God at any time,' John writes, but the Son 'has declared Him.' Jesus of Nazareth is God making Himself known — not a feeling, not a philosophy, but a person who walked real roads in a real province of the Roman Empire, was executed under a real governor, and left behind an empty tomb His enemies could never answer with a body.

That changes the question. 'Does God exist?' stops being an abstract debate and becomes something you can examine: read what Jesus said, weigh what He did, and decide who He is. If you want to know whether God exists — and what He's like — start there.
sections.scripture: No one has seen God at any time. The only begotten Son, who is in the bosom of the Father, He has declared Him.

quiz.question: According to John 1:18, how has the invisible God been made known?
options: Through the Son — Jesus Christ
Through human imagination
He hasn't — He stays hidden
quiz.reveal: No one has seen God at any time — but the Son 'has declared Him.' Jesus is God making Himself known: not an idea, but a person you can examine.

sections.heading: Why evidence isn't the real issue
sections.body:
Here's an honest question worth sitting with: if God's existence were proven to you beyond doubt tonight, would you follow Him tomorrow? For many of us, if we're truthful, the answer is no — because the debate was never really about evidence.

Jesus put His finger on it: the light has come into the world, and we 'loved darkness rather than light.' Not because the light was dim, but because light exposes. If God exists, He has a rightful claim on your life — your money, your relationships, your private habits, your pride. It is remarkably convenient for Him not to exist.

This isn't an accusation aimed at atheists. It's a confession about the human heart — yours and mine alike. Honest questions deserve honest answers, and there are good ones. But before you ask for more evidence, ask what you would do with the evidence you already have.
sections.scripture: And this is the condemnation, that the light has come into the world, and men loved darkness rather than light, because their deeds were evil. For everyone practicing evil hates the light and does not come to the light, lest his deeds should be exposed.

quiz.question: According to Jesus, why do people stay away from the light?
options: There isn't enough evidence
The light would expose their deeds
God makes Himself impossible to find
quiz.reveal: 'Men loved darkness rather than light, because their deeds were evil.' The obstacle is rarely the head. It's the heart — and that's true for all of us.

sections.heading: Test it honestly
sections.body:
God makes a promise to the honest seeker: 'You will seek Me and find Me, when you search for Me with all your heart.' Notice the condition. Not a casual glance. Not a debate you're trying to win. All your heart.

So test it. Read one of the Gospels — John is a good place to start — and ask honestly who this man is. Pray something as simple as this: 'God, if You're real, show me the truth, whatever it costs me.' If He isn't there, you've lost a few quiet minutes. If He is, you've found the reason you exist.

There's one more step for the honest. This site has a short test built on God's own Law — the standard your conscience already echoes. It won't prove God to you. It will show you where you stand before Him if He is there. And that, more than any argument, is where this question gets real.
sections.scripture: And you will seek Me and find Me, when you search for Me with all your heart.

faq.question: What's the strongest evidence that God exists?
faq.answer: Creation itself — the same everyday reasoning that tells you a building had a builder tells you the universe had a Maker. Paul writes that God's invisible attributes 'are clearly seen' in what He made, from the sun to the DNA in every cell, so clearly that no one can claim ignorance as an excuse. A second witness is closer still: your own conscience, which judges right and wrong by a standard you didn't invent.

faq.question: If God is real, why doesn't He just prove it beyond doubt?
faq.answer: Because the real obstacle usually isn't a lack of evidence but the human heart — Jesus said people 'loved darkness rather than light' because light exposes what they'd rather hide. If God's existence were proven tonight, many who ask for proof still wouldn't follow Him tomorrow, since a rightful claim on your life is what's actually at stake, not just an intellectual puzzle.

faq.question: How can I actually find out for myself if God is there?
faq.answer: God promises that the honest seeker will find Him: 'you will seek Me and find Me, when you search for Me with all your heart' — not a casual glance, but a real search. A practical starting point is reading one of the Gospels, praying honestly for the truth whatever it costs, and testing your life against God's own standard rather than debating in the abstract.

meta.title: Are You a Good Person? | Take the Test
meta.description: Most people think they're good. Find out where you really stand.

home.provocativeQuestion: If you died today, where will you spend eternity?
facts: Last year, 56 million people died worldwide.
Heart disease kills 17.9 million people every year.
Every 40 seconds, someone dies by suicide.
More people have died today than live in most towns.
The average life expectancy is 73 years. Then what?
150,000 people who woke up yesterday didn't wake up today.
Cancer claims 10 million lives every year.
Road accidents kill 1.35 million people annually.

reading.label: Daily bread
reading.descComplete: You've read the Word for all seven days.
reading.descActiveStart: Start with John 1. Seven days in the Word.

learn.label: Foundations
learn.descUpcoming: Study the foundations of the gospel.

share.label: Pass it on
share.descActive: You've walked the path. Someone you know hasn't.
share.descUpcoming: After you've walked the path yourself.

undecided.heading: You've seen the verdict. What will you do with it?

privacy.heading: Privacy Policy
privacy.description: How we handle your data on this site.

sections.title: What we collect
sections.body: We use PostHog for anonymous analytics (page views, button clicks, funnel completion) and Sentry for error tracking. Vercel Analytics collects anonymous performance metrics. We do not collect your name, email address, or any personally identifiable information.

sections.title: Cookies and local storage
sections.body: We do not use cookies. Your test progress, reading plan completion, and quiz answers are saved in your browser's local storage. This data never leaves your device and is not sent to any server.

sections.title: Analytics consent
sections.body: When you first visit, we ask whether you consent to analytics tracking. If you decline, PostHog is never loaded and no analytics data is collected. You can reset your consent choice at any time by clearing your browser data for this site.

sections.title: Third-party services
sections.body: PostHog (analytics), Sentry (error monitoring), and Vercel (hosting and performance) may process aggregated, anonymous data on their servers. We do not share any data with advertisers or sell data to third parties.

sections.title: Data retention
sections.body: Analytics data is retained according to PostHog's and Sentry's default retention policies. Local storage data persists until you clear your browser data or reset your progress within the app.

sections.title: Contact
sections.body: If you have questions about this privacy policy, you can reach us through the project's GitHub repository.

terms.heading: Terms of Use
terms.description: The terms that govern your use of this site.

sections.title: Purpose
sections.body: This site is a free educational and evangelistic resource. It presents the Christian gospel through an interactive moral examination, scripture-based teaching, and a guided reading plan.

sections.title: No professional advice
sections.body: The content on this site is not professional counselling, legal, medical, or psychological advice. If you are in crisis or need professional help, please contact a qualified professional or a crisis helpline.

sections.title: External links
sections.body: This site links to third-party resources (Bible reading plans, church directories, ministry websites). We are not responsible for the content, availability, or privacy practices of external sites.

sections.title: Intellectual property
sections.body: Scripture quotations are from publicly available Bible translations and are attributed where cited. The site's design, code, and original content are the property of the project's creators.

sections.title: No warranty
sections.body: This site is provided as-is. We make no guarantees about uptime, accuracy of translations, or completeness of content.

sections.title: Changes
sections.body: We may update these terms at any time. Continued use of the site constitutes acceptance of the updated terms.

about.heading: About
about.description: Who we are and why this site exists.

sections.title: Our mission
sections.body: This site exists to present the gospel of Jesus Christ clearly, honestly, and without compromise. We believe every person will stand before God and give an account of their life. Our goal is to help you consider that reality before it's too late.

sections.title: What we believe
sections.body: We hold to the historic Christian faith as revealed in the Bible. We believe that all people have sinned and fall short of God's glory, that the wages of sin is death, and that God demonstrates His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us. Salvation is by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone.

sections.title: The test
sections.body: The moral examination on this site is based on the Ten Commandments — God's moral standard. It is not designed to condemn, but to show every person their need for a Saviour. As the apostle Paul wrote: 'Through the law we become conscious of our sin' (Romans 3:20).

sections.title: Who built this
sections.body: This site was built by Christians who want to share the good news with as many people as possible. It is not affiliated with any specific church or denomination. It is a free resource and always will be.

blog.label: Blog
blog.indexSubtitle: Timely reflections on death, culture, and the hope of the gospel.
blog.saveStoryHint: Post it to your Instagram story, then add a link sticker pointing to this page.
blog.stickyUndecidedQuestion: You saw the verdict. It's still on the table.
blog.copyLinkCopied: Link copied — paste it into your story's link sticker.

footer.livingWatersLink: Are You a Good Person? — Living Waters
footer.scripture: For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life.
